zotero.abstractNote: High-contrast imaging is the primary technique for directly detecting exoplanets. However, the signal of a potential planet is typically buried under the much brighter light of the host star and the complex, time-varying speckle noise caused by the instrument and the atmosphere. To detect planets, one must model and subtract this noise. We propose a new approach to point spread function (PSF) modeling and subtraction based on half-sibling regression, a causal machine learning framework for removing noise from data. Our method, which we call half-sibling regression for exoplanet imaging (HSR-EI), is flexible, interpretable, and can incorporate domain knowledge about the imaging process. We demonstrate that HSR-EI is competitive with state-of-the-art PSF subtraction algorithms on real data from the VLT/SPHERE instrument, while offering a more principled approach to noise modeling. We also show that HSR-EI can be used to improve the performance of existing algorithms by providing a better estimate of the PSF.
